title: Anthropic publishes three-agent harness architecture for multi-hour autonomous development, separating planning, generation, and evaluation
summary: Anthropic published details of a three-agent harness design for long-running autonomous application development. The architecture divides autonomous development sessions into three distinct agents: a Planning Agent that establishes direction and task structure, a Generation Agent that produces code and design outputs, and an Evaluation Agent that assesses quality using few-shot examples and structured scoring criteria. The key innovation is separating the agent doing work from the agent judging it, addressing the documented problem of biased self-evaluation in single-agent systems. The system uses context resets with structured handoff artifacts between agents rather than context compaction, allowing each agent to continue from a well-defined state without losing coherence over multi-hour sessions (typically 5-15 iteration cycles, sometimes up to 4 hours). For frontend work, the evaluation agent applies four grading criteria: design quality, originality, craft, and functionality. According to Prithvi Rajasekaran, engineering lead at Anthropic Labs, this separation proves to be a strong lever for improving output quality in extended autonomous sessions.
whyItMatters: The plan-generate-evaluate separation establishes a reusable architectural pattern for autonomous development that engineering teams can adapt — organizations building internal agent workflows should study this design before reinventing their own orchestration patterns.

The context reset approach (structured handoff artifacts rather than context compaction) addresses one of the most practical limitations of current AI coding sessions: models becoming overly cautious or losing coherence as context windows fill, which currently limits agent autonomy to short tasks.

The explicit separation of generation from evaluation directly addresses the self-assessment bias documented in AI systems, where agents consistently overrate their own output — this has governance implications for any organization allowing agents to self-validate code before merge.
recommendedActions: Evaluate whether your current AI-assisted development workflows suffer from the self-evaluation bias this architecture addresses: if agents or AI tools are both generating and reviewing their own code, consider introducing independent evaluation checkpoints.
For teams experimenting with multi-agent development, adopt the plan-generate-evaluate separation as a baseline architecture and measure output quality compared to single-agent approaches.

Use the structured handoff artifact pattern for any long-running agent workflow (not just code generation) where context degradation over time is a risk — this applies to automated testing, documentation generation, and infrastructure management.
risksAndCaveats: The architecture is described through Anthropic's own use case and engineering blog — independent validation of quality improvements over single-agent systems at scale has not been published.

Multi-agent orchestration adds complexity, latency, and cost (three separate agent invocations per iteration cycle, 5-15 cycles per session) — the ROI depends on task complexity and may not justify overhead for shorter or simpler development tasks.

The evaluation criteria (design quality, originality, craft, functionality) are specific to frontend development; applicability to backend, infrastructure, or security-critical code generation may require significantly different evaluation frameworks.
